commit ed9a0454c53496580313e61497d322d7d9971bf6
Author: Sechang Lim <rhkrqnwk98@gmail.com>

mm/vma: fix memory leak in __mmap_region()

This commit attempts to fix a memory leak in the error path of
__mmap_region() when a swapped file is not released after a failure.

> diff --git a/mm/vma.c b/mm/vma.c
> index cc382217f730..1fab23446be3 100644
> --- a/mm/vma.c
> +++ b/mm/vma.c
> @@ -2857,6 +2857,8 @@ static unsigned long __mmap_region(struct file *file, unsigned long addr,
>  	if (map.charged)
>  		vm_unacct_memory(map.charged);
>  abort_munmap:
> +	if (map.file_doesnt_need_get && map.file)
> +		fput(map.file);

Could this result in a double-free if mmap_file() fails?

When .mmap_prepare swaps a file, map.file_doesnt_need_get is set to true
and map.file points to the new file. In __mmap_new_file_vma(), get_file()
is skipped.

If the subsequent mmap_file() call fails, the error-handling path
explicitly calls fput(vma->vm_file) to release the reference.

The error then propagates back to __mmap_region(), which jumps to the
abort_munmap label. Since map.file still points to the freed file pointer,
would this added fput() execute a second time on the same file?

Additionally, is there a file leak if the new mapping successfully merges
into an adjacent vma?

When a driver's .mmap_prepare returns a swapped file, it provides a new
reference and sets map.file_doesnt_need_get = true. If the new mapping is
adjacent to an existing vma that maps the exact same struct file pointer,
vma_merge_new_range() may successfully merge them.

On a successful merge, allocated_new becomes false, bypassing
__mmap_new_vma() where the reference is expected to be consumed.
__mmap_region() then returns the mapped address directly without dropping
the extra reference held in map.file.

Finally, does this miss a leak when call_action_prepare() fails?

In call_mmap_prepare(), vfs_mmap_prepare() is called first. If it allocates
a new file, it stores it in desc->vm_file with an initial reference.

call_action_prepare() is then invoked. If this action preparation fails,
the function returns early, skipping the assignments:

    map->file = desc->vm_file;
    map->file_doesnt_need_get = true;

When __mmap_region() jumps to abort_munmap, map.file_doesnt_need_get is
still false, so this fput(map.file) check is bypassed, leaking the newly
allocated file.

>  	vms_abort_munmap_vmas(&map.vms, &map.mas_detach);
>  	return error;
>  }

commit ed9a0454c53496580313e61497d322d7d9971bf6
Author: Sechang Lim <rhkrqnwk98@gmail.com>
Date:   Tue Mar 31 12:19:06 2026 +0000

    mm/vma: fix memory leak in __mmap_region()
    
    commit 605f6586ecf7 ("mm/vma: do not leak memory when .mmap_prepare
    swaps the file") handled the success path by skipping get_file() via
    file_doesnt_need_get, but missed the error path.
    
    When /dev/zero is mmap'd with MAP_SHARED, mmap_zero_prepare() calls
    shmem_zero_setup_desc() which allocates a new shmem file to back the
    mapping. If __mmap_new_vma() subsequently fails, this replacement
    file is never fput()'d - the original is released by
    ksys_mmap_pgoff(), but nobody releases the new one.
    
    Add fput() for the swapped file in the error path.
    
    Reproducible with fault injection.
    
    FAULT_INJECTION: forcing a failure.
    name failslab, interval 1, probability 0, space 0, times 1
    CPU: 2 UID: 0 PID: 366 Comm: syz.7.14 Not tainted 7.0.0-rc6 #2 PREEMPT(full)
    Hardware name: QEMU Ubuntu 24.04 PC v2 (i440FX + PIIX, arch_caps fix, 1996), BIOS 1.16.3-debian-1.16.3-2 04/01/2014
    Call Trace:
     <TASK>
     dump_stack_lvl+0x164/0x1f0
     should_fail_ex+0x525/0x650
     should_failslab+0xdf/0x140
     kmem_cache_alloc_noprof+0x78/0x630
     vm_area_alloc+0x24/0x160
     __mmap_region+0xf6b/0x2660
     mmap_region+0x2eb/0x3a0
     do_mmap+0xc79/0x1240
     vm_mmap_pgoff+0x252/0x4c0
     ksys_mmap_pgoff+0xf8/0x120
     __x64_sys_mmap+0x12a/0x190
     do_syscall_64+0xa9/0x580
     entry_SYSCALL_64_after_hwframe+0x76/0x7e
     </TASK>
    
    kmemleak: 1 new suspected memory leaks (see /sys/kernel/debug/kmemleak)
    BUG: memory leak
    unreferenced object 0xffff8881118aca80 (size 360):
      comm "syz.7.14", pid 366, jiffies 4294913255
      hex dump (first 32 bytes):
        00 00 00 00 ad 4e ad de ff ff ff ff 00 00 00 00  .....N..........
        ff ff ff ff ff ff ff ff c0 28 4d ae ff ff ff ff  .........(M.....
      backtrace (crc db0f53bc):
        kmem_cache_alloc_noprof+0x3ab/0x630

        alloc_file_pseudo+0x135/0x220
        __shmem_file_setup+0x274/0x420
        shmem_zero_setup_desc+0x9c/0x170
        mmap_zero_prepare+0x123/0x140
        __mmap_region+0xdda/0x2660
        mmap_region+0x2eb/0x3a0
        do_mmap+0xc79/0x1240

    Fixes: 605f6586ecf7 ("mm/vma: do not leak memory when .mmap_prepare swaps the file")
    Signed-off-by: Sechang Lim <rhkrqnwk98@gmail.com>

diff --git a/mm/vma.c b/mm/vma.c
index cc382217f730..1fab23446be3 100644
--- a/mm/vma.c
+++ b/mm/vma.c
@@ -2857,6 +2857,8 @@ static unsigned long __mmap_region(struct file *file, unsigned long addr,
 	if (map.charged)
 		vm_unacct_memory(map.charged);
 abort_munmap:
+	if (map.file_doesnt_need_get && map.file)
+		fput(map.file);
 	vms_abort_munmap_vmas(&map.vms, &map.mas_detach);
 	return error;
 }

    "thought": "**Analyzing a Memory Leak Fix in mmap_region**

Okay, so I'm looking at this patch that addresses a memory leak within `__mmap_region()`. The root cause is a missed `fput()` in the error path, specifically when the `.mmap_prepare` function swaps out the file.

The core of the issue is this: When mmap'ing `/dev/zero` with `MAP_SHARED`, `mmap_zero_prepare()` kicks off a file replacement via `shmem_zero_setup_desc()`. If `__mmap_new_vma()` fails *after* this replacement, the new, swapped-in file isn't released. The original file is handled by the caller, `ksys_mmap_pgoff()`, but the replacement goes rogue.

The patch's solution is simple: in the `abort_munmap:` error path of `__mmap_region()`, we're adding an `fput(map.file)` *if* `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is true (meaning the file was swapped) and `map.file` isn't NULL (which indicates that there was a swapped file). This looks like the right approach, but let's carefully consider it.

The intent is to clean up a swapped file in the error path. The code uses `mmap_state` named `map`. `map.file_doesnt_need_get` indicates that the file was swapped during `call_mmap_prepare()` and the new file already has a reference. Therefore, if we error out after `call_mmap_prepare()`, we need to `fput(map.file)` because the caller (`ksys_mmap_pgoff()`) only knows about the original `file` and will `fput()` that one, not the new one in `map.file`.

Hmm, is there any chance of the original `file` being leaked? No, the caller (`ksys_mmap_pgoff()`) is responsible for releasing the original `file`. The crucial point is that `__mmap_region` *doesn't* return the swapped `map.file` to the caller, it returns an address or an error code. Thus, the caller will not release it.

Now, let me confirm the references and lifetimes of the files involved. `call_mmap_prepare()` doesn't drop the original file; it just swaps it out. If `__mmap_region` *succeeds*, the new file (`map.file`) is attached to the VMA, so it gets an extra reference. And when `ksys_mmap_pgoff` succeeds, it will drop its reference to the original file. That is, the new file has its own reference and the original file is released. Therefore, everything is well-behaved.

However, if `__mmap_region` fails *after* the swap, the VMA is aborted, and the new `map.file` is left dangling. This is precisely the scenario the patch addresses by adding the necessary `fput`.

My assessment is that `fput(map.file)` in the error path is entirely correct, and it is safe. `map.file_doesnt_need_get` tells us that we *own* the reference. I have thoroughly reviewed the provided code. The error path, and therefore the resource leak, is covered in `abort_munmap:`.

I'm comfortable that this patch conceptually solves the resource leak in this error condition. I am not seeing any issues with this high-level conceptual change.

    "content": "{"content":"// SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-2.0-or-later\n\n/*\n * VMA-specific functions.\n */\n\n#include \"vma_internal.h\"\n#include \"vma.h\"\n\nstruct mmap_state {\n\tstruct mm_struct *mm;\n\tstruct vma_iterator *vmi;\n\n\tunsigned long addr;\n\tunsigned long end;\n\tpgoff_t pgoff;\n\tunsigned long pglen;\n\tunion {\n\t\tvm_flags_t vm_flags;\n\t\tvma_flags_t vma_flags;\n\t};\n\tstruct file *file;\n\tpgprot_t page_prot;\n\n\t/* User-defined fields, perhaps updated by .mmap_prepare(). */\n\tconst struct vm_operations_struct *vm_ops;\n\tvoid *vm_private_data;\n\n\tunsigned long charged;\n\n\tstruct vm_area_struct *prev;\n\tstruct vm_area_struct *next;\n\n\t/* Unmapping state. */\n\tstruct vma_munmap_struct vms;\n\tstruct ma_state mas_detach;\n\tstruct maple_tree mt_detach;\n\n\t/* Determine if we can check KSM flags early in mmap() logic. */\n\tbool check_ksm_early :1;\n\t/* If .mmap_prepare changed the file, we don't need to pin. */\n\tbool file_doesnt_need_get :1;\n};\n\n#define MMAP_STATE(name, mm_, vmi_, addr_, len_, pgoff_, vma_flags_, file_) \\\n\tstruct mmap_state name = {\t\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.mm = mm_,\t\t\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.vmi = vmi_,\t\t\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.addr = addr_,\t\t\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.end = (addr_) + (len_),\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.pgoff = pgoff_,\t\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.pglen = PHYS_PFN(len_),\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.vma_flags = vma_flags_,\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.file = file_,\t\t\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.page_prot = vma_get_page_prot(vma_flags_),\t\t\\\n\t}\n\n#define VMG_MMAP_STATE(name, map_, vma_)\t\t\t\t\\\n\tstruct vma_merge_struct name = {\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.mm = (map_)->mm,\t\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.vmi = (map_)->vmi,\t\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.start = (map_)->addr,\t\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.end = (map_)->end,\t\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.vma_flags = (map_)->vma_flags,\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.pgoff = (map_)->pgoff,\t\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.file = (map_)->file,\t\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.prev = (map_)->prev,\t\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.middle = vma_,\t\t\t\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.next = (vma_) ? NULL : (map_)->next,\t\t\t\\\n\t\t.state = VMA_MERGE_START,\t\t\t\t\\\n\t}\n\n/* Was this VMA ever forked from a parent, i.e. maybe contains CoW mappings? */\nstatic bool vma_is_fork_child(struct vm_area_struct *vma)\n{\n\t/*\n\t * The list_is_singular() test is to avoid merging VMA cloned from\n\t * parents. This can improve scalability caused by the anon_vma root\n\t * lock.\n\t */\n\treturn vma && vma->anon_vma && !list_is_singular(&vma->anon_vma_chain);\n}\n\nstatic inline bool is_mergeable_vma(struct vma_merge_struct *vmg, bool merge_next)\n{\n\tstruct vm_area_struct *vma = merge_next ? vmg->next : vmg->prev;\n\tvma_flags_t diff;\n\n\tif (!mpol_equal(vmg->policy, vma_policy(vma)))\n\t\treturn false;\n\n\tdiff = vma_flags_diff_pair(&vma->flags, &vmg->vma_flags);\n\tvma_flags_clear_mask(&diff, VMA_IGNORE_MERGE_FLAGS);\n\n\tif (!vma_flags_empty(&diff))\n\t\treturn false;\n\tif (vma->vm_file != vmg->file)\n\t\treturn false;\n\tif (!is_mergeable_vm_userfaultfd_ctx(vma, vmg->uffd_ctx))\n\t\treturn false;\n\tif (!anon_vma_name_eq(anon_vma_name(vma), vmg->anon_name))\n\t\treturn false;\n\treturn true;\n}\n\nstatic bool is_mergeable_anon_vma(struct vma_merge_struct *vmg, bool merge_next)\n{\n\tstruct vm_area_struct *tgt = merge_next ? vmg->next : vmg->prev;\n\tstruct vm_area_struct *src = vmg->middle; /* existing merge case. */\n\tstruct anon_vma *tgt_anon = tgt->anon_vma;\n\tstruct anon_vma *src_anon = vmg->anon_vma;\n\n\t/*\n\t * We _can_ have !src, vmg->anon_vma via copy_vma(). In this instance we\n\t * will remove the existing VMA's anon_vma's so there's no scalability\n\t * concerns.\n\t */\n\tVM_WARN_ON(src && src_anon != src->anon_vma);\n\n\t/* Case 1 - we will dup_anon_vma() from src into tgt. */\n\tif (!tgt_anon && src_anon) {\n\t\tstruct vm_area_struct *copied_from = vmg->copied_from;\n\n\t\tif (vma_is_fork_child(src))\n\t\t\treturn false;\n\t\tif (vma_is_fork_child(copied_from))\n\t\t\treturn false;\n\n\t\treturn true;\n\t}\n\t/* Case 2 - we will simply use tgt's anon_vma. */\n\tif (tgt_anon && !src_anon)\n\t\treturn !vma_is_fork_child(tgt);\n\t/* Case 3 - the anon_vma's are already shared. */\n\treturn src_anon == tgt_anon;\n}\n\n/*\n * init_multi_vma_prep() - Initializer for struct vma_prepare\n * @vp: The vma_prepare struct\n * @vma: The vma that will be altered once locked\n * @vmg: The merge state that will be used to determine adjustment and VMA\n *       removal.\n */\nstatic void &vp->remove;\n\n\tmemset(vp, 0, sizeof(struct vma_prepare));\n\tvp->vma = vma;\n\tvp->anon_vma = vma->anon_vma;\n\n\tif (vmg && vmg->__remove_middle) {\n\t\t*remove = vmg->middle;\n\t\tremove = &vp->remove2;\n\t}\n\tif (vmg && vmg->__remove_next)\n\t\t*remove = vmg->next;\n\n\tif (vmg && vmg->__adjust_middle_start)\n\t\tadjust = vmg->middle;\n\telse if (vmg && vmg->__adjust_next_start)\n\t\tadjust = vmg->next;\n\telse\n\t\tadjust = NULL;\n\n\tvp->adj_next = adjust;\n\tif (!vp->anon_vma && adjust)\n\t\tvp->anon_vma = adjust->anon_vma;\n\n\tVM_WARN_ON(vp->anon_vma && adjust && adjust->anon_vma &&\n\t\t   vp->anon_vma != adjust->anon_vma);\n\n\tvp->file = vma->vm_file;\n\tif (vp->file)\n\t\tvp->mapping = vma->vm_file->f_mapping;\n\n\tif (vmg && vmg->skip_vma_uprobe)\n\t\tvp->skip_vma_uprobe = true;\n}\n\n/*\n * Return true if we can merge this (vma_flags,anon_vma,file,vm_pgoff)\n * in front of (at a lower virtual address and file offset than) the vma.\n *\n * We cannot merge two vmas if they have differently assigned (non-NULL)\n * anon_vmas, nor if same anon_vma is assigned but offsets incompatible.\n *\n * We don't check here for the merged mmap wrapping around the end of pagecache\n * indices (16TB on ia32) because do_mmap() does not permit mmap's which\n * wrap, nor mmaps which cover the final page at index -1UL.\n *\n * We assume the vma may be removed as part of the merge.\n */\nstatic bool can_vma_merge_before(struct vma_merge_struct *vmg)\n{\n\tpgoff_t pglen = PHYS_PFN(vmg->end - vmg->start);\n\n\tif (is_mergeable_vma(vmg, /* merge_next = */ true) &&\n\t    is_mergeable_anon_vma(vmg, /* merge_next = */ true)) {\n\t\tif (vmg->next->vm_pgoff == vmg->pgoff + pglen)\n\t\t\treturn true;\n\t}\n\n\treturn false;\n}\n\n/*\n * Return true if we can merge this (vma_flags,anon_vma,file,vm_pgoff)\n * beyond (at a higher virtual address and file offset than) the as part of the merge.\n */\nstatic bool can_vma_merge_after(struct vma_merge_struct *vmg)\n{\n\tif (is_mergeable_vma(vmg, /* merge_next = */ false) &&\n\t    is_mergeable_anon_vma(vmg, /* merge_next = */ false)) {\n\t\tif (vmg->prev->vm_pgoff + vma_pages(vmg->prev) == vmg->pgoff)\n\t\t\treturn true;\n\t}\n\treturn false;\n}\n\nstatic void __vma_link_file(struct vm_area_struct *vma,\n\t\t\t    struct address_space *mapping)\n{\n\tif (vma_is_shared_maywrite(vma))\n\t\tmapping_allow_writable(mapping);\n\n\tflush_dcache_mmap_lock(mapping);\n\tvma_interval_tree_insert(vma, &mapping->i_mmap);\n\tflush_dcache_mmap_unlock(mapping);\n}\n\n/*\n * Requires inode->i_mapping->i_mmap_rwsem\n */\nstatic void __remove_shared_vm_struct(struct vm_area_struct *vma,\n\t\t\t\t address\n\t\t\t * space until vma start or end is updated.\n\t\t\t */\n\t\t\t__vma_link_file(vp->insert,\n\t\t\t\t\tvp->insert->vm_file->f_mapping);\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\n\tif (vp->anon_vma) {\n\t\tanon_vma_lock_write(vp->anon_vma);\n\t\tanon_vma_interval_tree_pre_update_vma(vp->vma);\n\t\tif (vp->adj_next)\n\t\t\tanon_vma_interval_tree_pre_update_vma(vp->adj_next);\n\t}\n\n\tif Note: current->mm is used for the search.\n *\n * @info: The unmapped area information including the range [low_limit -\n * high_limit), the alignment offset and mask.\n *\n * Return: A memory address or -ENOMEM.\n */\nunsigned long unmapped_area_topdown(struct vm_unmapped_area_info *info)\n{\n\tunsigned long length, gap, gap_end;\n\tunsigned long low_limit, high_limit;\n\tstruct vm_area_struct *tmp;\n\tVMA_ITERATOR(vmi, current->mm, 0);\n\n\t/* Adjust search length to account for worst case alignment overhead */\n\tlength = info->length + info->align_mask + info->start_gap;\n\tif (length < info->length)\n\t\treturn -ENOMEM;\n\n\tlow_limit = info->low_limit;\n\tif (low_limit < mmap_min_addr)\n\t\tlow_limit = mmap_min_addr;\n\thigh_limit = info->high_limit;\nretry:\n\tif (vma_iter_area_highest(&vmi, low_limit, high_limit, length))\n\t\treturn -ENOMEM;\n\n\tgap = vma_iter_end(&vmi) - info->length;\n\tgap -= (gap - info->align_offset) & info->align_mask;\n\tgap_end = vma_iter_end(&vmi);\n\ttmp = vma_next(&vmi);\n\t /* Avoid prev check if possible */\n\tif (tmp && vma_test_any_mask(tmp, VMA_STARTGAP_FLAGS)) {\n\t\tif (vm_start_gap(tmp) < gap_end) {\n\t\t\thigh_limit = vm_start_gap(tmp);\n\t\t\tvma_iter_reset(&vmi);\n\t\t\tgoto retry;\n\t\t}\n\t} else {\n\t\ttmp = vma_prev(&vmi);\n\t\tif (tmp && vm_end_gap(tmp) > gap) {\n\t\t\thigh_limit = tmp->vm_start;\n\t\t\tvma_iter_reset(&vmi);\n\t\t\tgoto retry;\n\t\t}\n\t}\n\n\treturn gap;\n}\n\n/*\n * Verify that the stack growth is acceptable and\n * update accounting. This is shared with both the\n * grow-up and grow-down cases.\n */\nstatic int acct_stack_growth(struct vm_area_struct *vma,\n\t\t\t     unsigned long size, unsigned long grow)\n{\n\tstruct mm_struct *mm = vma->vm_mm;\n\tunsigned long new_start;\n\n\t/* address space limit tests */\n\tif (!may_expand_vm(mm, &vma->flags, grow))\n\t\treturn -ENOMEM;\n\n\t/* Stack limit test */\n\tif (size > rlimit(RLIMIT_STACK))\n\t\treturn -ENOMEM;\n\n\t/* mlock limit tests */\n\tif (!mlock_future_ok(mm, vma_test(vma, VMA_LOCKED_BIT),\n\t\t\t     grow << PAGE_SHIFT))\n\t\treturn -ENOMEM;\n\n\t/* Check to ensure the stack will not grow into a hugetlb-only region */\n\tnew_start = vma->vm_end - size;\n#ifdef CONFIG_STACK_GROWSUP\n\tif (vma_test(vma, VMA_GROWSUP_BIT))\n\t\tnew_start = vma->vm_start;\n#endif\n\tif (is_hugepage_only_range(vma->vm_mm, new_start, size))\n\t\treturn -EFAULT;\n\n\t/*\n\t * Overcommit.. Okay, let's break this down systematically. I'm focusing on the `__mmap_region()` function and its associated logic, particularly concerning file descriptor management, which is critical.

Initially, `__mmap_region()` uses the `MMAP_STATE` macro to set up a `map` structure. Important point: even though `MMAP_STATE` doesn't *explicitly* initialize `file_doesnt_need_get`, because it is part of an initializer list, any missing fields are implicitly zero-initialized, so this starts as `false`.

Then `__mmap_setup` and `call_mmap_prepare` are called. `call_mmap_prepare()` is where the interesting part happens, the file swap.
If it *succeeds*, it eventually calls `call_mmap_prepare()`. If `desc->vm_file` and `map->file` are different, it means the file has been swapped, which is common in these situations. And if the swap happens, then `map->file_doesnt_need_get` is set to `true`, and `map->file` is set to the new file descriptor.

Now, if there is an error in `call_mmap_prepare()` or later functions, the code jumps to `abort_munmap`. The `abort_munmap` function is the safeguard to clean up.
It checks `map.file_doesnt_need_get`, and if it's true (meaning the file was swapped), it calls `fput(map.file)` to release the file.

However, I'm concerned about a potential edge case. If `call_mmap_prepare()` returns an error, *after* a file has been swapped, but before `map->file_doesnt_need_get` is set to `true`, then `fput` won't happen.

Specifically, look at `call_mmap_prepare()`: It invokes `vfs_mmap_prepare(map->file, desc)`. This is a *hook* into the filesystem's `mmap` operation.

Here's the problem: The call to `vfs_mmap_prepare()` can return an error! If `vfs_mmap_prepare()` fails, and the file has already been swapped within it, `map->file_doesnt_need_get` remains `false`.
And even if `vfs_mmap_prepare` *succeeds*, what if `call_action_prepare()` returns an error?
If the file is swapped as part of `vfs_mmap_prepare`, but then `call_action_prepare()` errors, the code will jump to `abort_munmap`, but the old file will be in `map->file`, and not the new, swapped file.

I need to see *how* `vfs_mmap_prepare` is swapping the file. More precisely, `vfs_mmap_prepare(map->file, desc)` calls `file->f_op->mmap_prepare(desc)`, which is where it could be done. The only way it could swap it is via a change to `desc->vm_file`.

The risk is a file descriptor leak if this sequence happens:

1.  `vfs_mmap_prepare` swaps `desc->vm_file`.
2.  `vfs_mmap_prepare` *itself* returns an error.
3.  `call_action_prepare` *succeeds* then returns an error.
4.  The cleanup code doesn't `fput` the new `desc->vm_file` because `file_doesnt_need_get` is still false.

    "thought": "**Analyzing a Potential File Descriptor Leak in the `mmap` Process**

Okay, so I'm tracing the `mmap` setup and I think I've spotted a potential file descriptor leak. Let's break this down. `shmem_zero_setup_desc()` allocates a file and assigns it to `desc->vm_file`. I remember now that earlier in the `mmap` process, `vfs_mmap_prepare(map->file, desc)` is called, and this internally calls `mmap_zero_prepare(desc)`, which in turn calls that `shmem_zero_setup_desc(desc)` that I mentioned before, and this sets `desc->vm_file`. So far so good, `vfs_mmap_prepare` returns 0 if successful.

Now, if `vfs_mmap_prepare` is successful, the next step is `call_action_prepare(map, desc)`. This function calls `mmap_action_prepare(desc)`. If *that* returns an error, `call_action_prepare` returns the error, which propagates back up to `call_mmap_prepare()`, and which then propagates back up to `__mmap_region`.

Here's the problem. In `__mmap_region`, if `call_mmap_prepare` *does* return an error, it jumps to `abort_munmap`. Inside `abort_munmap`, there's a critical conditional check: `if (map.file_doesnt_need_get && map.file) fput(map.file);`.

The issue is that if `call_action_prepare` fails, this does not set `map.file_doesnt_need_get` to TRUE. But the value of `desc->vm_file` has been updated by `shmem_zero_setup_desc()` by that point. This means that if `call_action_prepare` fails, `map.file_doesnt_need_get` remains FALSE, but `desc->vm_file` *does* point to the new file allocated in `shmem_zero_setup_desc()`. So, the `fput()` *won't* be called on that new `desc->vm_file`, leading to a file descriptor leak! The old `map.file` is not the one associated with the new `vm_file`, and it will likely be leaked.

I really need to dig into the `mmap_action_prepare` function to see if it *can* actually fail. If it can, this leak scenario is definitely a concern.

    "thought": "**Analysis of File Descriptor Swapping in `mmap` and Potential Memory Leaks**

Okay, let's break this down. My initial concern was a potential file descriptor leak if `call_action_prepare()` failed after a file swap. Specifically, if `call_action_prepare` returns an error, but `desc->vm_file` was already updated during an earlier file swap in the process. I hypothesized this could happen when drivers call `mmap_prepare`. I had to determine if there were specific code paths or conditions that lead to this.

I first considered the `/dev/zero` case, which uses a specific `MMAP_NOTHING` action.  However, this path is safe because `call_action_prepare` simply returns 0 in this scenario, and the `map->file_doesnt_need_get = true;` flag is correctly set. This means the system correctly accounts for the swapped file.

I then started thinking about a more generalized approach of how drivers might be written.  The core of the problem seems to be scenarios where `f_op->mmap_prepare` (the driver's mmap preparation routine) succeeds in modifying `desc->vm_file` to a "new" file but some subsequent stage fails. This "new" file might need an extra `fput` call.

I reviewed the commit message for the relevant patch. The commit directly addresses a leak where `__mmap_new_vma()` fails *after* a file swap. The patch adds `fput(map.file)` in the `abort_munmap` section, effectively releasing the new file. This part of the code correctly addressed the leak.

My next line of thought focused on potential issues within `mmap_region`. The crucial point became the interaction between `call_mmap_prepare` (which does the file swap) and the failure of either `mmap_action_complete` or `__mmap_new_vma`.

It appears that if `mmap_action_complete()` fails, it removes the new VMA by calling `do_munmap()` in a path where the refcount is managed, and we don't end up in `abort_munmap`. So, in this scenario, the file is properly cleaned up.

Then I considered the case where `vma_merge_new_range()` succeeds, which means we merge into an existing VMA. The patch specifically states "add fput(map.file) if __mmap_new_vma() fails." Then I understood that if `vma_merge_new_range()` returns a valid VMA, and we *merge* into an existing VMA, we don't create a new VMA. Thus `fput` is needed in the failure case where we *couldn't* merge the new mapping.

I further confirmed `vma_merge_new_range()` *requires* that the target VMA already has a file with the same pointer value.  If it succeeds, it extends the *existing* VMA, which *already* has a reference to the same `struct file`. However, in this case, the swapped file is *new* file. So merge should *always* fail.

This is the key. The new file comes with `map.file_doesnt_need_get = true;`, indicating that a reference has already been provided. `vma_merge_new_range` will always fail and thus `fput(map.file)` is needed if `__mmap_new_vma()` or `vma_merge_new_range()` fails. If either one fails, it is correct that we need to release the file descriptor.

I then began to think about drivers that call `mmap_prepare` that replace `desc->vm_file` with the *same* file that already exists in the merge target VMA (if it exists). In this rare situation, and `vma_merge_new_range()` succeeds, we don't have a leak.

The key takeaways are:
*   The original patch addressed the core issue.
*   If we can't merge, we have to make the new VMA. We then check if the VMA can be made. This is fine.
*   If the system *can't* create a new VMA, the code falls back to `abort_munmap`, and we *do* need `fput(map.file)`. This part of the code is fine.
*   If `mmap_action_complete()` fails, it releases the file, which is good.
*   The merging case is safe; the swapped files are new and cannot be merged because `is_mergeable_vma` requires the same file descriptor.

In summary, the patch

    "content": "{"content":"commit 605f6586ecf78395f0185ab24c368fb46a06e434\nAuthor: Lorenzo Stoakes <ljs@kernel.org>\nDate:   Mon Jan 12 15:51:43 2026 +0000\n\n    mm/vma: do not leak memory when .mmap_prepare swaps the file\n    \n    The current implementation of mmap() is set up such that a struct file\n    object is obtained for the input fd in ksys_mmap_pgoff() via fget(), and\n    its reference count decremented at the end of the function via.  fput().\n    \n    If a merge can be achieved, we are fine to simply decrement the refcount\n    on the file.  Otherwise, in __mmap_new_file_vma(), we increment the\n    reference count on the file via get_file() such that the fput() in\n    ksys_mmap_pgoff() does not free the now-referenced file object.\n    \n    The introduction of the f_op->mmap_prepare hook changes things, as it\n    becomes possible for a driver to replace the file object right at the\n    beginning of the mmap operation.\n    \n    The current implementation is buggy if this happens because it\n    unconditionally calls get_file() on the mapping's file whether or not it\n    was replaced (and thus whether or not its reference count will be\n    decremented at the end of ksys_mmap_pgoff()).\n    \n    This results in a memory leak, and was exposed in commit ab04945f91bc\n    (\"mm: update mem char driver to use mmap_prepare\").\n    \n    This patch solves the problem by explicitly tracking whether we actually\n    need to call get_file() on the file or not, and only doing so if required.\n    \n    Link:     "role": "assistant",
    "thought": "**Analysis of Potential Reference Leak in `mmap_prepare` and `vma_merge_new_range()`**

Okay, so I'm digging into this old code, specifically the interaction between `vma_merge_new_range()` and how file references are handled after a successful `mmap_prepare` swap. My initial thought is: what happens to the file if the merge *succeeds*? The core idea I'm tracking is around reference counting and potential leaks.

If `vma_merge_new_range()` merges the new mapping with an existing one, it doesn't create a new VMA. Instead it extends the current one. The original file's `fput()` would be handled at a higher level (like `ksys_mmap_pgoff()`). But what happens if `.mmap_prepare()` swaps in a *new* file? Could we have a situation where the new file is "lost"?

Let me trace it. When `ksys_mmap_pgoff()` is called, it calls `fget()`, passes to `do_mmap()`, then `mmap_region()`, and then `__mmap_region()`. `vfs_mmap_prepare()` swaps `desc->vm_file` to a new file (say, `/dev/zero`), the new file has a refcount of 1.  Then `call_mmap_prepare()` sets `map->file_doesnt_need_get = true` and `map->file = new_file`.

Now, we try to merge with existing VMAs using `vma_merge_new_range(&vmg)`. If that succeeds, the VMA's `vm_file` must match the new file. But `new_file` is new, and not cached, so it can't match unless a driver caches files. The merge *should* always fail in the normal case of `shmem_zero_setup_desc()`. But let's look at the scenario of a merge success with a cached `struct file`. The cached file means `vma_merge_new_range()` *succeeds*.

If the merge is successful, `__mmap_new_vma()` *isn't* called, but `__mmap_complete()` is. Then we call `ksys_mmap_pgoff()`, and then `fput(old_file)`. But what about the *new* file that the driver provided?  Did the driver give a reference to this new file? Yes, but `__mmap_new_vma()` is skipped, the driver must have returned a reference! If `vma_merge_new_range()` succeeds, no new VMA is created. The new reference is LEAKED!

So, the driver passes ownership, and if a new VMA is created, it's correct to skip `get_file()`. However, if the mapping *merges* into an existing VMA, that existing VMA already *has* a reference. That means that `__mmap_region` MUST `fput(new_file)` if it successfully merges! `vma_merge_new_range` doesn't drop the reference itself.

Let's go back to `__mmap_region`. If `vma` is found, meaning merge succeeded, we skip `__mmap_new_vma`. The driver provided reference `map.file` has `map.file_doesnt_need_get = true` and that means *we* own the reference now. But we didn't put it in a new VMA. And we don't `fput(map.file)` on the success path! The reference is leaking!

The patch adds `fput()` *only* in the error path. If the merge succeeds, there is no `fput()`.

I'm thinking, can `vma_merge_new_range()` ever succeed if `file_doesnt_need_get == true`? It *could* if the driver returns a cached file, and that's the vulnerability I need to test.  Are there any drivers that do that? Let's check `mmap_prepare` implementations.

    "thought": "**Analysis of Linux Kernel `mmap` File Leak Vulnerability**

My investigation centers on a potential file descriptor leak within the Linux kernel's `mmap` implementation, specifically when handling file-backed memory mappings and the potential for file swapping during the `mmap_prepare` phase. I've focused on scenarios where file swapping occurs in `vfs_mmap_prepare`, like with `/dev/zero`, and where errors arise after the swap.

I see that several drivers (`ext4`, `orangefs`, `f2fs`, `ocfs2`) modify `desc->vm_ops` but don't swap the file pointer (`desc->vm_file`). The critical path involves `mmap_zero_prepare` in `/dev/zero`. This driver *does* change `desc->vm_file` using `shmem_zero_setup_desc()`. I've also examined how the `MAP_SHARED` flag, VMA merging, and the usage of `/dev/zero` interact in relation to these code paths.

My primary concern centers around the case where `mmap_action_complete()` fails after a file swap, and the failure is handled incorrectly, leading to a file descriptor leak, which is a key assumption of the syzbot report. However, I confirmed that in the case `mmap_action_complete()` fails, `do_munmap` is called, which handles the necessary `fput` calls, and there is no leak.

However, a critical vulnerability exists when `__mmap_new_vma()` fails *after* a file swap in `call_mmap_prepare()`. This could occur if the system runs out of memory (OOM). In this scenario, the newly allocated file referenced by `desc->vm_file` is not `fput()` because the logic to release the swapped file pointer is not triggered after the error.

I've traced how the error handling interacts with the `map.file_doesnt_need_get` flag, confirming it's only set in the successful swap case. When `__mmap_new_vma()` fails in the scenario after `call_mmap_prepare`, but before the reference counting logic to `vma->vm_file`, the swapped file pointer will have `map.file_doesnt_need_get` set to true, preventing the necessary cleanup. The patch that adds an `fput()` to the `abort_munmap` error path is therefore correct. I've confirmed that the patch adds a single `fput` call in the error path. I also checked that the patch correctly addresses the OOM case.

I have verified that the `fput(map.file)` is correct because `shmem_zero_setup_desc()` allocates the file with refcount 1. I also investigated other locations where `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is used and how the reference is transferred to a VMA.

My investigation confirms the validity of the reported file descriptor leak in the outlined scenario and that the provided patch appropriately addresses the OOM condition, preventing the leak.

Okay, let's break this down meticulously. My initial thought process focused on error handling within `__mmap_new_vma` and the `__mmap_region` function. I initially traced how errors like `-ENOMEM` from `vm_area_alloc` would propagate, eventually leading to `unacct_error` and the correct `fput(map.file)` call when `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is true. The patch seemed sound at first glance.

Then I shifted my focus to `abort_munmap`. I verified that both failures within `__mmap_new_vma` (like those stemming from `vma_start_write_killable()`) and failures during completion of the `mmap_action_complete()` call lead to `abort_munmap`, correctly calling `fput` if needed. I also tracked all places `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is set to true to ensure complete understanding of this flag's usage.

The critical insight, however, came when I examined `__mmap_new_file_vma`. Here's where the problem manifests. If `mmap_file()` fails *within* `__mmap_new_file_vma`, it *already* calls `fput(vma->vm_file)`, and critically, `vma->vm_file` *was* `map->file`. The error propagates back to `__mmap_region` via `__mmap_new_vma`, which leads to `abort_munmap`. But the flag `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is *still* set to true. Therefore, `abort_munmap` ends up calling `fput(map.file)` *again*, leading to a double-free because `map.file` was already released by the failed `mmap_file()` operation.

Therefore, `__mmap_new_vma` does call `__mmap_new_file_vma` and now my focus will be on the exact call stack, to verify precisely when `__mmap_file` can fail, to produce the double-free.

    "thought": "**Critical Double-Free Vulnerability in Kernel Memory Mapping**

Okay, here's what I've found. This is a serious issue. I've been dissecting the `__mmap_new_vma` function in the kernel and discovered a critical double-free vulnerability, triggered by a specific error handling scenario.

Here's the breakdown: the `__mmap_new_file_vma` function is used when a file is mapped. If the file's `.mmap()` operation fails (e.g., driver returns -ENOMEM or -EINVAL), this function attempts to clean up its resources, including calling `fput()` on the file. However, after calling `fput(vma->vm_file)`, it also *incorrectly* calls `fput(map.file)` again, leading to a double free on the same `struct file` pointer. The `map.file_doesnt_need_get` flag is to blame here!

The root cause: the patch attempts to fix a syzkaller OOM report, but the fix introduces a double free if `__mmap_new_file_vma` fails.

This double free happens because the flag `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is still true in the `abort_munmap` function. The patch author should have:
1. Made `__mmap_new_file_vma` set `map->file_doesnt_need_get = false` after consuming the reference.
OR
2. Made `__mmap_new_file_vma` set `map->file = NULL`.

The vulnerability can be triggered via `shmem_mmap` (for `/dev/zero` shared mappings), if `shmem_mmap` fails.

After an extensive analysis, I also found that in this error path, it can also lead to a memory leak in the driver's mmap_prepare if a merge happens.

In short, there's a serious double-free vulnerability that's easy to trigger, and a potential memory leak that's currently unreachable, both related to how the kernel handles file mappings and error paths. This needs immediate attention.

      "reasoning": "When a driver swaps the file via .mmap_prepare, map.file_doesnt_need_get is set to true. If VMA allocation succeeds but the file's f_op->mmap callback fails inside __mmap_new_file_vma(), that function explicitly calls fput(vma->vm_file) to drop the file reference. The error is then propagated back to __mmap_region(), which jumps to abort_munmap. The newly added code there checks `if (map.file_doesnt_need_get && map.file)` and calls fput(map.file) again. Since map.file was never cleared and points to the same file, this triggers a double-free on the struct file."

      "description": "The swapped file reference is leaked if the new mapping successfully merges into an existing VMA.",
      "reasoning": "If a driver's .mmap_prepare callback swaps the file for a cached/shared one that matches an adjacent VMA, vma_merge_new_range() will successfully extend the existing VMA and bypass new VMA allocation. The adjacent VMA already holds its own reference to the file, making the new reference acquired during .mmap_prepare (and held in map.file) unneeded. However, in this success path, __mmap_region() simply returns the address without calling fput(map.file) to drop the extra reference, permanently leaking it. While currently unreachable because /dev/zero allocates a unique shmem file that cannot merge, this fundamentally breaks the API contract for any driver returning a cached file."
    },

      "reasoning": "In call_mmap_prepare(), if vfs_mmap_prepare() successfully replaces desc->vm_file with a new file but the subsequent call_action_prepare() returns an error, the function exits early. Because map->file and map->file_doesnt_need_get are only updated after call_action_prepare(), they retain their original values. When __mmap_region() receives the error and jumps to abort_munmap, it skips fput(map.file) because map.file_doesnt_need_get is false, causing the newly allocated file trapped in desc->vm_file to be permanently leaked."

Okay, I need to review this commit addressing a memory leak in `__mmap_region()`. The message states the problem occurs when `mmap_prepare` swaps out the original file, but the error path misses freeing the new file if `__mmap_new_vma()` fails.

Let's dive into the code. The patch adds `fput(map.file)` within `abort_munmap`, but only if `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is true.  I have to think about this... the `abort_munmap` is the error path.

Looking at `__mmap_region()`, specifically the `abort_munmap` label, that's where the patch is. I need to understand the context. Tracing the execution, it appears we're going through `call_mmap_prepare()`.

```c
err = call_action_prepare(map, desc);
	if (err)
		return err;

	/* Update fields permitted to be changed. */
	map->pgoff = desc->pgoff;
	if (desc->vm_file != map->file) {
		map->file_doesnt_need_get = true;
		map->file = desc->vm_file;
	}
```

Okay, the `call_mmap_prepare()` function updates `map->file`, and also sets `map.file_doesnt_need_get = true` if the file is swapped. That's key. So, the original `map->file` came from `struct file *file` that was passed into `__mmap_region()`. The caller is `mmap_region` or `do_mmap`.

The commit message says the *original* file is released by `ksys_mmap_pgoff()`. Hmm. So, the original file has a reference taken via `fget()`. The new file doesn't have a ref.

If we hit an error *after* the file swap, but before the new VMA is created, `__mmap_region()` returns an error.  The call stack will unwind back up to `ksys_mmap_pgoff`, which has a reference to the *original* file. `ksys_mmap_pgoff` calls `fput()` on the *original* file. But, we also want to release the new file.

Now, if `map.file` was swapped to a new file, and we call `fput(map.file)` in the error path, this drops the reference to the new file. This resolves the memory leak!

Let's consider success. If `__mmap_region` succeeds, `__mmap_new_vma()` and friends will increment the reference count on the new file, unless `file_doesnt_need_get` is set. If the file doesn't need a get, the VMA inherits the reference to the new file without `get_file()`. Then the VMA owns the reference to the *new* file. So, the original file is released. Then, `ksys_mmap_pgoff` will STILL call `fput()` on the original file! Which is *correct*. Because, the original file's reference from `fget` is no longer needed.

If `__mmap_region` fails, the VMA isn't created. The new file's reference is leaked. The added patch, `fput(map.file)` on the error path, if `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is true, *drops* the reference to the new file. This is the fix.

Aha! Is there any edge case where `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is set, but the error path is triggered before the file swap? No. They're set together in `call_mmap_prepare`.

Alright. I'm satisfied. This patch is correct. It cleans up the potentially leaked file when the `mmap_prepare` function replaces the file, and mmap fails. I'll give this a thumbs up.

Okay, so I'm digging into how `mmap` works, particularly with the new `mmap_prepare` mechanism, and I've hit something interesting. But here's the kicker: what happens if `mmap_prepare` sets an action *and* the VMA gets merged?

Looking at the code, it tries to merge VMAs first. If it can't, then a new VMA is allocated, and the `mmap_action_complete()` function is *always* called with this new VMA. But, if the VMA *is* merged... `allocated_new` is false, and `mmap_action_complete()` is NEVER called. This is a problem! Take `MMAP_REMAP_PFN` for instance. That maps physical frames into the VMA. If we merge with an existing VMA, does the new range get mapped? It's looking like it doesn't! The new range is unmapped because `mmap_action_complete()` isn't called! That's a huge bug! We *must* map the new physical frames.

If it was merged, `mmap()` was still called on the VMA! Did it call `mmap()` on the *merged* VMA? Yes! And even historically, `mmap_region` called `vma->vm_file->f_op->mmap(file, vma)` on the NEW VMA.

Historically, `mmap_region()` allocated a NEW VMA and then called `file->f_op->mmap(file, vma)`. The code would then try to merge the VMA *after* the `mmap()` call. Wait! In the current kernel (post rewrite of `mmap_region`), the merge attempt was moved to BEFORE the `mmap()` call or VMA allocation! This move creates the bug I'm seeing!

    "thought": "**My Analysis of VMA Merging in `mmap`**

Alright, the critical point seems to be `desc.action`, which dictates the course of events following an `mmap`. However, `vma_merge_struct` doesn't directly expose an 'action' field, so I need to trace how it's handled. My initial hypothesis is that if `mmap_prepare()` sets `desc->action.type`, say to `MMAP_REMAP_PFN`, and we're extending an existing VMA (Virtual Memory Area), the existing VMA gets stretched. But I need to be careful; what if `is_mergeable_vma` returns true and the mapping merges? 

Let me clarify something. Does `/dev/mem` swaps the file? No, it does not. Therefore, if I do an `mmap` on `/dev/mem` with an address adjacent to an existing `/dev/mem` mapping with identical permissions, there's a strong likelihood it'll merge. If the merge happens, `allocated_new` will be `false`. Crucially, if there's a merge, `mmap_action_complete(vma, &desc.action)` doesn't get called! This is where things get interesting. This implies the new pages for the merged portion aren't mapped into the page table via `remap_pfn_range`! I need to be sure. Does `vma_merge_new_range` handle the action?

No, I don't think so. `vma_merge_new_range` is only concerned with VMA flags, offsets, and the file, and doesn't deal with `desc.action`. It acts on `vmg.vma_flags`, `vmg.pgoff`, `vmg.file`, etc. only. Therefore, the essential call to `mmap_action_complete()` is bypassed! I'd better revisit the code of `__mmap_region` again to verify these assumptions.

    "thought": "**My Analysis of `VM_SPECIAL` and VMA Merging**

Okay, hold on a second. I initially thought that the `VM_SPECIAL` flag would *always* block VMA merging, but I'm not seeing that explicitly stated anywhere. Let me dig a little deeper... Ah, here it is: `is_mergeable_vma` actually checks `vma_flags_diff_pair()`. So, the merge *happens* if the flags are IDENTICAL! That's the key.

So, two adjacent `mmap` calls on the *same* file with the exact same flags, including `VM_SPECIAL`, are, in fact, mergable. And if they *are* merged, `allocated_new` becomes `false`. This, in turn, skips `mmap_action_complete()`, which is a problem.

If the driver used `mmap_prepare` and set `desc.action.type = MMAP_REMAP_PFN`, and the VMAs get merged, then `remap_pfn_range_complete` is never invoked! This is a significant issue because that means the new mapping range will not have page tables and hence won't be usable.

Wait, if it *is* merged, it's really just extending an existing VMA. Does extending a `VM_PFNMAP` VMA somehow trigger remapping of the PFNs automatically? No, it doesn't!

Okay, new angle...what if the `desc.action.type` *is* `MMAP_REMAP_PFN`? Does `mmap_action_prepare()` prevent merging in that case? I think I need to trace through the `remap_pfn_range_prepare()` logic.

    "thought": "**Analyzing a Potential Regression in `mmap` with Merging**

Okay, let's break this down. My initial thought process is centered around how `mmap` behaves with `/dev/zero` and the implications of VMA merging, especially with the introduction of `mmap_prepare`.

First, I realize that if `mmap_zero_private_success()` isn't called, `vma_set_anonymous` doesn't get executed. That's a key observation. Then, I remember if `vma_merge_new_range` succeeds, we're dealing with `is_mergeable_anon_vma` and `is_mergeable_vma` tests. If the previous VMA was anonymous, `is_mergeable_vma` checks `vmg->file == vma->vm_file`.

And here's the crucial point: `/dev/zero` *does* call `vma_set_anonymous()`, and this sets `vma->vm_ops = NULL;`, which signifies an anonymous VMA. However, even with `vma->vm_ops` as NULL, the `vma->vm_file` still points to `/dev/zero`!

So, when we call `mmap` on `/dev/zero`, `__mmap_region` sets `vmg.file = file;` (which is `/dev/zero`), and the *existing* VMA also has `vma->vm_file = /dev/zero`. This is critical. Thus, `is_mergeable_vma` returns TRUE (since `vma->vm_file == vmg->file`) and the VMAs *merge*. This means that `mmap_zero_private_success` is *skipped*. Does this matter? Well, for the merged VMA, `vma->vm_ops` is already NULL, and the new VMA is also "anonymous".

Now I am asking myself, does `__mmap_region` overwrite `vma->vm_ops`? That's when I recall the `set_vma_user_defined_fields()` function. It appears that if `map->vm_ops` is not NULL, `vma->vm_ops` *is* overwritten. But `mmap_zero_prepare` sets it to NULL. But what if the driver sets this? I think if the driver sets `desc->vm_ops`, and the VMAs merge, the merge *overwrites* `vma->vm_ops` with the new VMA's `map->vm_ops`.

Here's the problem: if it's merged, the *new* range doesn't get `remap_pfn_range_complete`! Now, my mind flashes back to the old logic. The old `mmap()` always called `mmap()` on the new VMA, and that invoked `remap_pfn_range()` *before* any merging. If `remap_pfn_range` is now skipped, the page tables aren't populated. That's a regression! This looks like a regression introduced by `mmap_prepare`.

Now I need to verify if `do_remap_pfn_range` is ever called if `allocated_new` is false. Yes! I'm seeing that it is *NOT* called. `mmap_action_complete()` is the *only* place `remap_pfn_range_complete` is called. And `mmap_action_complete()` is skipped if `allocated_new == false`. So, it's NOT handled elsewhere. It's skipping the page table population step.

Now, let's get precise. Let's look at the actual diff, especially around where `mmap.file` is handled: there is a `map.file_doesnt_need_get` now!

So, the critical point is that `mmap_action_complete()` is ONLY called if `allocated_new` is true. Why did the author add `&& allocated_new`? That is what is causing the page table population to be skipped, in certain situations, which is a regression. Let me check the original commit that introduced `mmap_prepare`. Let me check the history around `mm/vma.c` around `mmap_action_complete`.

    "content": "{"output":"commit ac0a3fc9c07df79dc8a4ce9d274df00afc7bf12d\nAuthor: Lorenzo Stoakes <ljs@kernel.org>\nDate:   Mon Oct 20 13:11:27 2025 +0100\n\n    mm: add ability to take further action in vm_area_desc\n    \n    Some drivers/filesystems need to perform additional tasks after the VMA is\n    set up.  This is typically in the form of pre-population.\n    \n    The forms of pre-population most likely to be performed are a PFN remap\n    or the insertion of normal folios and PFNs into a mixed map.\n    \n    We start by implementing the PFN remap functionality, ensuring that we\n    perform the appropriate actions at the appropriate time - that is setting\n    flags at the point of .mmap_prepare, and performing the actual remap at the\n    point at which the VMA is fully established.\n    \n    This prevents the driver from doing anything too crazy with a VMA at any\n    stage, and we retain complete control over how the mm functionality is\n    applied.\n    \n    Unfortunately callers still do often require some kind of custom action,\n    so we add an optional success/error _hook to allow the caller to do\n    something after the action has succeeded or failed.\n    \n    This is done at the point when the VMA has already been established, so\n    the harm that can be done is limited.\n    \n    The error hook can be used to filter errors if necessary.\n    \n    There may be cases in which the caller absolutely must hold the file rmap\n    lock until the operation is entirely complete. It is an edge case, but\n    certainly the hugetlbfs mmap hook requires it.\n    \n    To accommodate this, we add the hide_from_rmap_until_complete flag to the\n    mmap_action type. In this case, if a new VMA is allocated, we will hold the\n    file rmap lock until the operation is entirely completed (including any\n    success/error hooks).\n    \n    Note that we do not need to update __compat_vma_mmap() to accommodate this\n    flag, as this function will be invoked from an .mmap handler whose VMA is\n    not yet visible, so we implicitly hide it from the rmap.\n    \n    If any error arises on these final actions, we simply unmap the VMA\n    altogether.\n    \n    Also update the stacked filesystem compatibility layer to utilise the\n    action behaviour, and update the VMA tests accordingly.\n    \n    While we're here, rename __compat_vma_mmap_prepare() to __compat_vma_mmap()\n    as we are now performing actions invoked by the mmap_prepare in addition to\n    just the mmap_prepare hook.\n    \n    Link: https://lkml.kernel.org/r/2601199a7b2eaeadfcd8ab6e199c6d1706650c94.1760959442.git.lorenzo.stoakes@oracle.com\n    Signed-off-by: Lorenzo Stoakes <lorenzo.stoakes@oracle.com>\n    Cc: Alexander Gordeev <agordeev@linux.ibm.com>\n    Cc: Al Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>\n    Cc: Andreas Larsson <andreas@gaisler.com>\n    Cc: Andrey Konovalov <andreyknvl@gmail.com>\n    Cc: Arnd Bergmann <arnd@arndb.de>\n    Cc: Baolin Wang <baolin.wang@linux.alibaba.com>\n    Cc: Baoquan He <bhe@redhat.com>\n    Cc: Chatre, Reinette <reinette.chatre@intel.com>\n    Cc: Christian Borntraeger <borntraeger@linux.ibm.com>\n    Cc: Christian Brauner <brauner@kernel.org>\n    Cc: Dan Williams <dan.j.williams@intel.com>\n    Cc: Dave Jiang <dave.jiang@intel.com>\n    Cc: Dave Martin <dave.martin@arm.com>\n    Cc: Dave Young <dyoung@redhat.com>\n    Cc: David Hildenbrand <david@redhat.com>\n    Cc: David S.     "thought": "Here's my take on the commit message and the potential implications:

**Analyzing the VMA Unmap Strategy**

Okay, so the commit message highlights a fail-safe approach: if there's an error during those final operations, the Virtual Memory Area (VMA) gets completely unmapped. The commit emphasizes that this is done *after* the VMA is already set up, which, at first glance, suggests a mitigation of potential damage. The logic here is pretty standard, you're not going to be making the system less stable by attempting to handle errors after they already exist. The fact that the scope of the potential harm is limited due to timing makes sense from a resource allocation perspective.

However, the commit message's focus on unmapping completely neglects any mention of merging. This omission makes me raise my eyebrows a little. Given that we are dealing with memory management operations, and VMAs in particular, not mentioning the potential need or implications of merging VMAs seems like a significant oversight. To get a clearer picture of how these "final actions" are integrated with existing VMAs, I should probably check the diff for the `__mmap_region` function itself.     "content": "{"content":" * do some brk-specific accounting here.\n */\nint do_brk_flags(struct vma_iterator *vmi, struct vm_area_struct *vma,\n\t\t unsigned long addr, unsigned long len, vm_flags_t vm_flags)\n{\n\tstruct mm_struct *mm = current->mm;\n\n\t/*\n\t * Check against address space limits by the changed size\n\t * Note: This happens *after* clearing old mappings in some code paths.\n\t */\n\tvm_flags |= VM_DATA_DEFAULT_FLAGS | VM_ACCOUNT | mm->def_flags;\n\tvm_flags = ksm_vma_flags(mm, NULL, vm_flags);\n\tif (!may_expand_vm(mm, vm_flags, len >> PAGE_SHIFT))\n\t\treturn -ENOMEM;\n\n\tif (mm->map_count > sysctl_max_map_count)\n\t\treturn -ENOMEM;\n\n\tif (security_vm_enough_memory_mm(mm, len >> PAGE_SHIFT))\n\t\treturn -ENOMEM;\n\n\t/*\n\t * Expand the existing vma if possible; Note that singular lists do not\n\t * occur after forking, so the expand will only happen on new VMAs.\n\t */\n\tif (vma && vma->vm_end == addr) {\n\t\tVMG_STATE(vmg, mm, vmi, addr, addr + len, vm_flags, PHYS_PFN(addr));\n\n\t\tvmg.prev = vma;\n\t\t/* vmi is positioned at prev, which this mode expects. */\n\t\tvmg.just_expand = true;\n\n\t\tif (vma_merge_new_range(&vmg))\n\t\t\tgoto out;\n\t\telse if (vmg_nomem(&vmg))\n\t\t\tgoto unacct_fail;\n\t}\n\n\tif (vma)\n\t\tvma_iter_next_range(vmi);\n\t/* create a vma struct for an anonymous mapping */\n\tvma = vm_area_alloc(mm);\n\tif (!vma)\n\t\tgoto unacct_fail;\n\n\tvma_set_anonymous(vma);\n\tvma_set_range(vma, addr, addr + len, addr >> PAGE_SHIFT);\n\tvm_flags_init(vma, vm_flags);\n\tvma->vm_page_prot = vm_get_page_prot(vm_flags);\n\tvma_start_write(vma);\n\tif (vma_iter_store_gfp(vmi, vma, GFP_KERNEL))\n\t\tgoto mas_store_fail;\n\n\tmm->map_count++;\n\tvalidate_mm(mm);\nout:\n\tperf_event_mmap(vma);\n\tmm->total_vm += len >> PAGE_SHIFT;\n\tmm->data_vm += len >> PAGE_SHIFT;\n\tif (vm_flags & VM_LOCKED)","end_line":2860,"start_line":2800,"total_lines":3244}",

    "content": "{"output":"commit efa7df3e3bb5da8e6abbe37727417f32a37fba47\nAuthor: Rik van Riel <riel@surriel.com>\nDate:   Thu Dec 14 14:34:23 2023 -0800\n\n    mm: align larger anonymous mappings on THP boundaries\n    \n    Align larger anonymous memory mappings on THP boundaries by going through\n    thp_get_unmapped_area if THPs are enabled for the current process.\n    \n    With this patch, larger anonymous mappings are now THP aligned.     "thought": "**Analyzing Potential File Reference Leaks in Memory Mapping**

Okay, so I'm thinking through this memory mapping logic. The core issue is, if `vma->vm_file != vmg->file`, the merge will fail, which makes sense. It's designed to merge identical files, that is, when the files are the same. But that means if they *are* identical, the existing VMA already *holds* a reference to that file. We're essentially mapping more pages from the same file.

But then, the `file_doesnt_need_get` flag throws a wrench into this. If this flag is true, it means `mmap_prepare` created a *brand new* `struct file`. No other part of the kernel has a reference to this new file, right? So how could the existing `vma->vm_file` possibly be the *same* as the file we just created in `mmap_prepare`? It can't!

Aha, that makes sense. Because, for example, `shmem_zero_setup_desc` allocates a *new* file. A new file is impossible to merge with an existing VMA. This also means `vma_merge_new_range` will *always* fail and return `NULL`. This scenario means that `allocated_new` will be `true`. Okay, so at least in this case, the `vma_merge_new_range` function won't leak the file because it knows it's new.

But here's a wrinkle: what if `mmap_prepare` *swaps* to a *pre-existing* file and returns that? Does it return it with a *new* reference? We're setting `desc->vm_file` with the new file. If the answer is "yes, it returns a new reference" and `file_doesnt_need_get` is true, then if the VMA *merges* with an existing VMA, `__mmap_region` returns *without* freeing that extra reference. Because `allocated_new` is false, and it just returns the address. The `map.file_doesnt_need_get && map.file` check is ONLY in the `abort_munmap` error path!

Alright, let's step through `__mmap_region` carefully again:

```c
	if (!vma) {
		error = __mmap_new_vma(&map, &vma, &desc.action);
		if (error)
			goto unacct_error;
		allocated_new = true;
	}

	if (have_mmap_prepare)
		set_vma_user_defined_fields(vma, &map);

	__mmap_complete(&map, vma);

	if (have_mmap_prepare && allocated_new) {
		error = mmap_action_complete(vma, &desc.action);
		if (error)
			return error;
	}

	return addr;
```

If the VMA merges (i.e., `vma` is non-NULL from `vma_merge_new_range`), it skips `__mmap_new_vma`, sets fields, completes, and *returns the address*. It *never* `fputs` the `map.file`, even though we got a *new* reference!

Hold on... if it merges, the VMA is expanded. The VMA's existing `vm_file` *already* holds a reference. But `mmap_prepare` gave us *another* reference. That extra reference... *that's the potential leak!*

So, the key question is, are there *any* `mmap_prepare` implementations that return a pre-existing file? I need to check the code. Maybe something in the `fs/overlayfs/file.c` or similar? Let's go look at how `mmap_prepare` is *actually* implemented.

    "thought": "**Analysis of the `mmap_prepare` Patch: Identifying a Critical Use-After-Free Vulnerability**

Okay, here's the deal. I've been digging into this patch related to `mmap_prepare`, especially focusing on how it handles `/dev/zero` (`mmap_zero_prepare`). The commit message mentioned a specific scenario, and I've traced it down to identify a serious issue: a Use-After-Free (UAF) condition that can arise when `mmap_file` fails after the file swap has occurred.

Here's my thought process, broken down:

The patch introduces a new file (`new_file`) using `shmem_zero_setup_desc(vma)` for the `/dev/zero` mapping. The crucial step is when `desc->vm_file` gets set to `new_file`, and then `map->file` (the original file from sys_mmap) gets updated to point to this `new_file`. This is the file swap, and now we need to manage the refcount.

The original patch assumes that any error *before* the file swap has been addressed and that the code's error handling for cases where `vm_area_alloc` fails is correct, since it decrements the refcount of `new_file` from 1 to 0 and thus frees it.

However, the problem arises if `vm_area_alloc` succeeds and `__mmap_new_file_vma` is subsequently called. This function *should* handle the `mmap_file` failure. And it does: it calls `fput(vma->vm_file)`, decrementing the refcount of `new_file` to zero and freeing the memory.

Here's the rub: if `mmap_file` fails (let's say it returns -EINVAL), the code goes through the error handling path correctly. The new file is freed. But then, it returns an error to `__mmap_region`. The `__mmap_region` then jumps to the `abort_munmap` function. The function's logic calls `fput(map.file)`. But `map.file` is the now-dangling `new_file`! This results in a double-free, a definite UAF bug.

The patch author was correct to put the condition `if (map.file_doesnt_need_get && map.file)`. It means that `map.file` is not NULL and thus needs to be freed. The problem is that the condition is always TRUE because `new_file` is already freed by the failing `__mmap_new_file_vma`!

So, in the case of a `mmap_file` failure *after* the file swap, the patch introduces a double free: `fput` in `__mmap_new_file_vma` *and* `fput` in `abort_munmap`, leading to memory corruption.

I did, of course, have to check for additional error conditions. If `mmap_action_complete()` fails, the patch behaves correctly. The `do_munmap` call handles the file decrement.

The root of the issue is that `__mmap_region` is *still* assuming the swapped file (`new_file`) is valid when it reaches `abort_munmap` on some errors. It's not taking into account that the swapped file *might* have already been released by `__mmap_new_file_vma` if `mmap_file` failed.

The potential fix is to set `map->file = NULL` or `map->file_doesnt_need_get = false;` in `__mmap_new_file_vma` in the error path before the call to `fput` so it's not double-freed later on. The only thing that `__mmap_new_file_vma` is responsible for is releasing *its* reference to the file, and that's it.

    "content": "{"content":"// SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-2.0\n/*\n *  linux/fs/ext4/file.c\n *\n * Copyright (C) 1992, 1993, 1994, 1995\n * Remy Card (card@masi.ibp.fr)\n * Laboratoire MASI - Institut Blaise Pascal\n * Universite Pierre et Marie Curie (Paris VI)\n *\n *  from\n *\n *  linux/fs/minix/file.c\n *\n *  Copyright (C) 1991, 1992  Linus Torvalds\n *\n *  ext4 fs regular file handling primitives\n *\n *  64-bit file support on 64-bit platforms by Jakub Jelinek\n *\t(jj@sunsite.ms.mff.cuni.cz)\n */\n\n#include <linux/time.h>\n#include <linux/fs.h>\n#include <linux/iomap.h>\n#include <linux/mount.h>\n#include <linux/path.h>\n#include <linux/dax.h>\n#include <linux/filelock.h>\n#include <linux/quotaops.h>\n#include <linux/uio.h>\n#include <linux/mman.h>\n#include <linux/backing-dev.h>\n#include \"ext4.h\"\n#include \"ext4_jbd2.h\"\n#include \"xattr.h\"\n#include \"acl.h\"\n#include \"truncate.h\"\n\n/*\n * Returns %true if the given DIO request should be attempted with DIO, or\n * %false if it should fall back to buffered I/O.\n *\n * DIO isn't well specified; when it's unsupported (either due to the request\n * being misaligned, or due to the file not supporting DIO at all), filesystems\n * either fall back to buffered I/O or return EINVAL.  For files that don't use\n * any special features like encryption or verity, ext4 has traditionally\n * returned EINVAL for misaligned DIO.  iomap_dio_rw() uses this convention too.\n * In this case, we should attempt the DIO, *not* fall back to buffered I/O.\n *\n * In contrast, in cases where DIO is unsupported due to ext4 features, ext4\n * traditionally falls back to buffered I/O.\n *\n * This function implements the traditional ext4 behavior in all these cases.\n */\nstatic bool ext4_should_use_dio(struct kiocb *iocb, struct iov_iter *iter)\n{\n\tstruct inode *inode = file_inode(iocb->ki_filp);\n\tu32 dio_align = ext4_dio_alignment(inode);\n\n\tif (dio_align == 0)\n\t\treturn false;\n\n\tif (dio_align == 1)\n\t\treturn true;\n\n\treturn IS_ALIGNED(iocb->ki_pos | iov_iter_alignment(iter), dio_align);\n}\n\nstatic ssize_t ext4_dio_read_iter(struct kiocb *iocb, struct iov_iter *to)\n{\n\tssize_t ret;\n\tstruct inode *inode = file_inode(iocb->ki_filp);\n\n\tif (iocb->ki_flags & IOCB_NOWAIT) {\n\t\tif (!inode_trylock_shared(inode))\n\t\t\treturn -EAGAIN;\n\t} else {\n\t\tinode_lock_shared(inode);\n\t}\n\n\tif (!ext4_should_use_dio(iocb, to)) {\n\t\tinode_unlock_shared(inode);\n\t\t/*\n\t\t * Fallback to buffered I/O if the operation being performed on\n\t\t * the inode is not supported by direct I/O. The IOCB_DIRECT\n\t\t * flag needs to be cleared here in order to ensure that the\n\t\t * direct I/O path within generic_file_read_iter() is not\n\t\t * taken.\n\t\t */\n\t\tiocb->ki_flags &= ~IOCB_DIRECT;\n\t\treturn generic_file_read_iter(iocb, to);\n\t}\n\n\tret = iomap_dio_rw(iocb, to, &ext4_iomap_ops, NULL, 0, NULL, 0);\n\tinode_unlock_shared(inode);\n\n\tfile_accessed(iocb->ki_filp);\n\treturn ret;\n}\n\n#ifdef CONFIG_FS_DAX\nstatic ssize_t ext4_dax_read_iter(struct kiocb *iocb, struct iov_iter *to)\n{\n\tstruct inode *inode = file_inode(iocb->ki_filp);\n\tssize_t ret;\n\n\tif (iocb->ki_flags & IOCB_NOWAIT) {\n\t\tif (!inode_trylock_shared(inode))\n\t\t\treturn -EAGAIN;\n\t} else {\n\t\tinode_lock_shared(inode);\n\t}\n\t/*\n\t * Recheck under inode lock - at this point we are sure it cannot\n\t * change anymore\n\t */\n\tif (!IS_DAX(inode)) {\n\t\tinode_unlock_shared(inode);\n\t\t/* Fallback to buffered IO in case we cannot support DAX */\n\t\treturn generic_file_read_iter(iocb, to);\n\t}\n\tret = dax_iomap_rw(iocb, to, &ext4_iomap_ops);\n\tinode_unlock_shared(inode);\n\n\tfile_accessed(iocb->ki_filp);\n\treturn ret;\n}\n#endif\n\nstatic ssize_t ext4_file_read_iter(struct kiocb *iocb, struct iov_iter *to)\n{\n\tstruct inode *inode = file_inode(iocb->ki_filp);\n\n\tif (unlikely(ext4_forced_shutdown(inode->i_sb)))\n\t\treturn -EIO;\n\n\tif (!iov_iter_count(to))\n\t\treturn 0; /* skip atime */\n\n#ifdef CONFIG_FS_DAX\n\tif (IS_DAX(inode))\n\t\treturn ext4_dax_read_iter(iocb, to);\n#endif\n\tif (iocb->ki_flags & IOCB_DIRECT)\n\t\treturn ext4_dio_read_iter(iocb, to);\n\n\treturn generic_file_read_iter(iocb, to);\n}\n\nstatic ssize_t ext4_file_splice_read(struct file *in, loff_t *ppos,\n\t\t\t\t     struct pipe_inode_info *pipe,\n\t\t\t\t     size_t len, unsigned int flags)\n{\n\tstruct inode *inode = file_inode(in);\n\n\tif (unlikely(ext4_forced_shutdown(inode->i_sb)))\n\t\treturn -EIO;\n\treturn filemap_splice_read(in, ppos, pipe, len, flags);\n}\n\n/*\n * Called when an inode is released. Note that this is different\n * from ext4_file_open: open gets called at every open, but release\n * gets called only when /all/ the files are closed.\n */\nstatic int ext4_release_file(struct inode *inode, struct file *filp)\n{\n\tif (ext4_test_inode_state(inode, EXT4_STATE_DA_ALLOC_CLOSE)) {\n\t\text4_alloc_da_blocks(inode);\n\t\text4_clear_inode_state(inode, EXT4_STATE_DA_ALLOC_CLOSE);\n\t}\n\t/* if we are the last writer on the inode, drop the block reservation */\n\tif ((filp->f_mode & FMODE_WRITE) &&\n\t\t\t(atomic_read(&inode->i_writecount) == 1) &&\n\t\t\t!EXT4_I(inode)->i_reserved_data_blocks) {\n\t\tdown_write(&EXT4_I(inode)->i_data_sem);\n\t\text4_discard_preallocations(inode);\n\t\tup_write(&EXT4_I(inode)->i_data_sem);\n\t}\n\tif (is_dx(inode) && filp->private_data)\n\t\text4_htree_free_dir_info(filp->private_data);\n\n\treturn 0;\n}\n\n/*\n * This tests whether the IO in question is block-aligned or not.\n * Ext4 utilizes unwritten extents when hole-filling during direct IO, and they\n * are converted to written only after the IO is complete.  Until they are\n * mapped, these blocks appear as holes, so dio_zero_block() will assume that\n * it needs to zero out portions of the start and/or end block. Okay, so I've been digging into this `mmap` code, and I've found a couple of critical issues. Let's break this down. First, there's only one path that seems to swap the `vm_file` in the `mmap_prepare` stage: `shmem_zero_setup_desc`. However, the root problem here is that `__mmap_new_file_vma` can, and *does*, drop a reference count on a file. This can lead to double frees if not handled correctly.

The main concern is a **Double Free / Use-After-Free** scenario in the error handling path of `__mmap_new_file_vma`. The patch introduces an unconditional `fput` in `abort_munmap`, but the conditions surrounding file swapping are not carefully considered in the context of `mmap_file` failures.

Here's the scenario: 
1.  If the `.mmap_prepare` hook, called by `mmap_prepare()`, swaps the file (which sets `map.file_doesnt_need_get` to true and `map.file` to a new file), and then the subsequent `mmap_file()` fails within `__mmap_new_file_vma`, then `vma->vm_file` will have the file.
2.  `__mmap_new_file_vma` now runs the error path, which includes `fput(vma->vm_file)`. This drops the *only* reference to the new file (so far, so good).
3.  The error is propagated to `__mmap_region` which jumps to `abort_munmap`.
4.  The patch *unconditionally* calls `fput(map.file)` *if* `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is true. `map.file` still points to the *already freed* file, this will cause a double free (or a UAF).

To clarify, a potential fix would be to set `map->file = NULL;` in the error path of `__mmap_new_file_vma`. If `__mmap_new_file_vma` frees the file in error handling, it's responsible for clearing `map->file`.

Another issue to consider is a **Memory Leak** when `call_action_prepare` fails.
If `call_action_prepare()` fails, the code jumps to `abort_munmap`, but `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is still false. So `fput(map.file)` is skipped. If `vfs_mmap_prepare` has already swapped in a *new* file, that file leaks because `desc->vm_file` is the local variable that goes out of scope and nobody `fput`s it. This could leak any file allocated (e.g., from `mmap_zero_prepare`).

So, there are two separate problems: a double-free on `.mmap` failure, and a memory leak on `call_action_prepare` failure. I think it would be best to separate the `file_doesnt_need_get` logic for error path cleanup. And the error handling for `vfs_mmap_prepare` must be revised.

    "thought": "**Analysis of mmap File Leak in Kernel Patch**

Okay, so I've been digging into this `do_munmap()` process. It's clear that the existing `fput(vma->vm_file)` call during VMA destruction neatly handles file reference counting in the typical scenario. The returning `error` in `__mmap_region` from that path is perfectly fine.

I'm now focusing on the patch itself and its handling of potential failures in `call_mmap_prepare()`. Specifically, I'm thinking about what happens if `call_mmap_prepare()` fails *after* `vfs_mmap_prepare()` succeeds. The crucial point here is that `vfs_mmap_prepare()` *might* allocate a *new* file for `desc->vm_file` (like `/dev/zero` via `shmem_zero_setup_desc()`).

If, in this situation, `call_action_prepare()` fails, the function returns with error, and we jump to `abort_munmap`. `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is false, and the newly allocated `desc->vm_file` would be leaked because `fput` is not called. I'm wondering if `call_action_prepare()` can fail with `/dev/zero`, but it looks like the action is not set or that the prepare call is successful with that driver, making it safe.

However, I'm concerned that other drivers could potentially switch out `desc->vm_file` and introduce a failing action. It's a latent bug waiting to happen, even if there are no examples now.

Looking at what happens if `__mmap_new_vma` fails... in that scenario, `map.file_doesnt_need_get` IS true, and `map.file` is indeed the swapped file. The patch correctly handles this path, which is good.

Now, let's explore `__mmap_region` itself. It looks like if `__mmap_setup` fails, we jump to `abort_munmap`, and again, no `fput` is called. That makes sense. The same logic from the previous scenario may apply, where `desc.vm_file` might have been changed to a newly allocated file, leading to the leak. It appears to be a very subtle and rare bug, but a valid one nonetheless.

Now, I'm scrutinizing the patch logic again, particularly the `abort_munmap` part:

If `map.file_doesnt_need_get` was true *before* `__mmap_new_vma()` and `__mmap_new_vma` fails, is `map.file` guaranteed to be the swapped file? Yes, it's set in `call_mmap_prepare`. This path seems correct.

I'm thinking about VMA merging now. If `vma_merge_new_range` succeeds, is there an issue? If `vma_merge_new_range` merges the new region into an existing VMA, does that affect the file reference count?

The VMA merging process *does* take a file reference because, well, the existing VMA already has a reference to the file. If VMA merging happens, it modifies an *existing* VMA, which already has its reference count handled. It does NOT call `get_file()` and so no leak. The patch appears sound in this specific case.

    "thought": "**My Analysis of the Linux Kernel's `mmap` Implementation**

Okay, here's the breakdown of my deep dive into the `mmap` code, focusing on the tricky file reference management:

My initial thought was that a call to `vma_merge_new_range(&vmg)` expands an existing VMA. If this happens via `__mmap_region()`, a new VMA isn't created. My concern was about the fate of the file reference in this scenario. Then, I dug into `is_mergeable_vma()`. This check is critical, because it determines if a newly mapped region can merge with an adjacent VMA. The key condition is whether the `vm_file` of the adjacent VMA matches the file being mapped. If they match, `vma_merge_new_range` succeeds!

The crux of the problem arises when the file is "swapped" during `call_mmap_prepare()`, such as with `/dev/zero` using `shmem_zero_setup_desc()`. This function allocates a *new* shmem file and assigns it to `desc->vm_file`. If a brand new file is created, it'll never merge with an adjacent VMA. Therefore, `vma_merge_new_range` will always return `NULL` when `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is true (because the file is newly allocated). When `map.file_doesnt_need_get` is false, and `vma_merge_new_range` succeeds, then `vma` is non-NULL. The kernel then returns, and the old file is dropped in the caller.

However, if a driver *did* replace `desc->vm_file` with an existing file (and increment its reference count) in `mmap_prepare`, `vma_merge_new_range` *could* succeed, expanding an adjacent VMA. In this case, `allocated_new` would be false. The problem, as I identified, is that in this scenario the newly swapped file doesn't get attached to the VMA! This means the driver's extra reference is leaked! Currently, this is not happening because no drivers return an existing file. They *always* swap to a *new* file.

I also looked at the error handling in `ksys_mmap_pgoff`, and made sure the file reference count is handled correctly even when a `mmap_action_complete()` fails, `do_munmap()` unmaps the VMA and calls `fput(vma->vm_file)`. I also saw that `fput(map.file)` is safe before `vms_abort_munmap_vmas()` because overlapped VMAs have their OWN `vm_file`, and don't use `map.file`. If the process fails with `-ENOMEM`, then the VMAs are permanently gone.

I paid close attention to the `call_mmap_prepare()` function. If this fails, the file reference becomes problematic. If `call_mmap_prepare` fails, the `map.file_doesnt_need_get` remains FALSE, and `map->file` remains the old file. This means there is a leak, because `desc` is on the stack, and when it goes out of scope, the newly allocated file is leaked. I tested this by calling `shmem_zero_setup_desc()`. I confirmed this using the `search_file_content` results. I then determined this is only triggered if a driver calls `call_action_prepare()` *after* `desc->vm_file` is set, and it is NOT currently happening.

I then went through multiple other potential failure paths (failure of `update_ksm_flags`, `set_vma_user_defined_fields`, `__mmap_new_vma`, etc.). I noticed in `__mmap_new_vma` calls `vm_area_free(vma)` if an allocation fails. I verified `vm_area_free(vma)` *does* properly drop the file reference via `fput(vma->vm_file)`. Thus, no file leak. I also verified the same is true if the VMA is already allocated, but unmapping fails.

Finally, I concluded that, although currently not *triggerable* by existing drivers, the logic where `call_mmap_prepare` fails and swaps the file is a potential source of a file reference leak, because the code does not drop the reference in the failure path. This anti-pattern should be addressed, although the current implementation doesn't have an instance of the problem.

Okay, so `map.file_doesnt_need_get` starts as false, which is correct. However, this flag gets set to true within `call_mmap_prepare()`, and I need to understand the implications of this.  Let's break down the logic. `ksys_mmap_pgoff()` always does `fput(file)` on the original file if there is one.

If `mmap_prepare` (e.g. `shmem_zero_setup_desc`) allocates a new file, it returns it with a refcount of 1.  `call_mmap_prepare` then replaces the original `map.file` with the new one and sets `map.file_doesnt_need_get` to true.  If an error occurs later and we hit `abort_munmap`, the code executes: `if (map.file_doesnt_need_get && map.file) fput(map.file);`. That seems correct because `call_mmap_prepare` replaced the `map.file`.

Now, if things succeed, and `mmap_action_complete` runs, the code does not call `fput(map.file)`. So, who's responsible for the new file? The VMA, the VMA holds the reference. And the code in `__mmap_new_file_vma` confirms this:  `vma->vm_file = map->file;` and if `map->file_doesnt_need_get` is true, the `get_file` is NOT called. That makes sense, because that single reference, returned by `mmap_prepare`, is taken by the VMA.

This is exactly what commit 605f6586ecf7 ("mm/vma: do not leak memory when .mmap_prepare swaps the file") intended: to handle the success path by skipping `get_file()` and using `file_doesnt_need_get`.

But let's think about failure scenarios *after* `__mmap_new_file_vma`. The code flows like so:

```c
	if (vma->vm_file)

	else
		error = __mmap_new_vma(&map, &vma, &desc.action);

	if (error)
		goto unacct_error;
```
If `__mmap_new_file_vma` fails, it returns an error, which leads to `unacct_error`, which branches to `abort_munmap`. So then, at the `abort_munmap`, `fput` is called!

But wait, what happens if `__mmap_new_file_vma` succeeds, but there's a *later* failure?  I need to check the rest of the code of `__mmap_region` because that looks like the only scenario which would cause problems. It doesn't look like there are any failure scenarios after `__mmap_new_file_vma` in `__mmap_region`.

So, it does look as though the commit, correctly identifies the path and avoids the leak, but I need to make sure I am correct, so I will analyze the failure paths more thoroughly.

My concern flares up when I see that `mmap_action_complete` can fail *after* the VMA has already been merged or allocated and inserted into the VM tree, and after `__mmap_complete` has been called! The code just returns the `error` directly!

The immediate issue I'm seeing is that if `mmap_action_complete` fails, we bypass the error cleanup and unmapping! The VMA is left in a partially constructed state, which is very dangerous. So it would appear that the kernel effectively has a "successful" mmap at this point because `ksys_mmap_pgoff` will return this `error` instead of an address.

Okay, if `__mmap_region` returns an error after `mmap_action_complete`, it bails immediately. `mmap_action_complete` can fail, in which case `do_munmap()` is called. `do_munmap()` calls `remove_vma()`, which calls `fput(vma->vm_file)`.
